mappings.rationaleAI: The Cold War's superpower rivalry directly structures A.3 (Truman Doctrine, Marshall Plan, NATO, containment policy) and A.3.b (Eisenhower's foreign policy), and frames every subsequent US administration's foreign policy through A.5. The section cannot be understood without this context.

A.2.d covers US wartime mobilisation, the role of US forces in defeating the Axis, and postwar occupation of Japan and Germany; B.2 covers Canadian participation including manpower mobilisation and the development of armed forces.

Canada is the direct subject of the entire B division, covering the interwar Liberal government under King, WWII participation, postwar foreign policy in NATO and the UN, Commonwealth relations, Franco-Canadian tensions, and postwar economic expansion.

The United States is the direct subject of the entire A division from the 1920 Republican administrations through the George W. Bush era, covering every presidential administration, domestic policy, and foreign affairs outlined.

The Great Depression is explicitly named in A.1.c (Hoover's administration, stock market crash, economic repercussions) and B.1.b (Depression's impact on Canada, Conservative relief measures 1930–35).

The Soviet Union is the defining antagonist of the Cold War sections A.3 onward—Truman's containment policy, Eisenhower's détente, Nixon's SALT talks, Reagan's Gorbachev-era relations, and détente under Ford all require coverage of Soviet policies and actions.

Vietnam is explicitly named in A.4.b.iii (Gulf of Tonkin, escalation of US involvement), A.4.c.i (Paris peace talks, Cambodia invasion, ceasefire 1973), and A.4.d (Ford administration conclusion of the war 1975).

NATO's creation is explicitly named in A.3.a.i (Truman Doctrine and NATO formation) and is the framework for Eisenhower's military alliances in A.3.b; Canada's participation in NATO is outlined in B.3.a as a cornerstone of postwar foreign policy.

This article spans both divisions of section 973, covering 20th-century US and Canadian history including the World Wars, the postwar boom, the Vietnam era, and late-20th-century political and economic developments.

Henry Ford's assembly-line mass production and the five-dollar workday are emblematic of the Republican-era 'Coolidge prosperity' and favouritism toward big business described in A.1.a; his Model T and Fordism defined US industrial culture of the 1920s.

South Korea is the direct subject of the Korean War (A.3.a.iv), including US and UN intervention (1950–53), which the outline lists as a key event of the Truman administration and a major Cold War confrontation.

Iran appears in three distinct outline nodes: the US-sponsored coup of 1953 (A.3.b.ii under Eisenhower's foreign policy), the Iranian hostage crisis during Carter (A.4.e), and the Iran-Contra Affair under Reagan (A.4.f.i).

China figures in US foreign policy across multiple outline nodes: US support of Nationalist China under Truman (A.3.a.i), Nixon's rapprochement with the People's Republic and opening of diplomatic relations (A.4.c.i), and continuation of China relations through the Clinton era (A.5.d).

Stalin is the Soviet counterpart who shaped the Cold War framework that defines A.3; his postwar Eastern Bloc formation and the ideological competition he embodied directly drove the Truman Doctrine, Marshall Plan, and NATO creation outlined in A.3.a.i.

The United Kingdom is central to Canada's section: B.1.a covers Commonwealth relations under King's Liberal government, B.3.a addresses postwar Canadian participation in NATO alongside the UK and Commonwealth affairs, and B.3.b covers Canada's involvement in British Commonwealth matters.
